chapters.title: A new way of thinking can heal the deepest fights
chapters.text:
The book tells a quiet story about people caught in painful conflict. You recognize yourself in the characters and feel the weight of their struggle. Hidden inside the story are sharp lessons that push you to grow into a kinder, stronger person.

The world shifts when you choose to look past the surface of a fight and reach for what lies underneath. Real human power goes beyond a change in actions. It starts with knowing your neighbor without fear.

Peace in any moment depends on the choices you make, not on what other people do. The decision rests in your hands.

The path you pick sets you up for war or for peace. When you twist issues of race, religion, and color into something bitter, you build a need to defend yourself. That need grows into war inside your heart and with the world around you.

People who lead teams, raise children, or care for others should study these ideas. The book shows that a peaceful life rests on compassion and shared humanity. Without those, your heart may fight all day, whether you are at home or at the office. The clear tools in this summary give you a way forward.

Your way of being must show care and responsibility. When leaders make that change at the core, the care their group gives to others rises. You start to see people as people, not as objects. Old conflicts that have lasted for years can finally end.

You will read about parenting, managing workers, peace talks in the Middle East, and personal growth. This book is a life raft for anyone suffering in silence and drowning in fear.

The world will look different if you are willing to practice these ideas and weave them into daily life.

chapters.title: Bad habits feed the fighting all around us
chapters.text:
These days, relationships between people and between groups seem to break apart faster than ever. It feels urgent to learn how to mend what is tearing and how to keep a thread of connection when people pull away. Families split over political views. Disputes at work bleed into the home, and home fights steal your focus at the office.

A 2013 Stanford University survey of executive coaches found that CEOs ranked conflict management as the most important skill. From families to nations, people agree that we must learn to handle fights well. Even with that clear need, many feel unsure about how to begin. We stay stuck because we carry wrong ideas about what conflict really is.

Fights drag on because each side waits for the other to move first.

All conflict flows from the same source. When we cannot see that source, we feed the very fire we are trying to put out.

The book offers a tested method for the problem of conflict wrapped in a story. Two men who had lost family members to each other found a way to live in peace and help others find peace in their lives. We can find the same kind of release from what weighs us down. A better family, a better group, and a better society all wait inside the challenges we face.

chapters.title: Change yourself first and you spark change in others
chapters.text:
A simple drawing called the Influence Pyramid suggests putting effort into making things go well rather than fixing what is broken. Yet we flip that pyramid when we try to settle our personal and work disagreements. We try to fix our children, change our partners, correct our employees, and punish anyone who steps out of line. But most problems need more than correction to fade. If your main goal in talking to people is to make them change, you will not move them.

You become an agent of change by living in a way that helps things go right, not by just fixing what is wrong.

To help things go right, you need a heart that is at peace. Consider the long fight over Jerusalem as an example. The city began as a Jewish place and stayed at the center of bitter conflict for centuries. Two big forces, the Western Crusaders and Muslim armies, occupied the land in turns. Both sides committed terrible acts against the other and against the original residents. Yet one Turkish military leader stood apart. His name was Saladin. His army took Jerusalem back in 1187. Historians often credit his success to luck, but a deeper reason sits behind his military and political wins. Saladin’s calm heart was the source of his triumph.

The state of your heart toward others is the single most important factor in any conflict.

While other forces looted the city and hauled away goods, Saladin told his men not to harm a single Christian or take their property. He showed humanity toward his enemies and his captives.

In every meeting with another person, you choose to see them as a human or as an object. That choice shapes how you treat them. When they are objects, you want to fix them. When they are humans, your heart rests easy with them. They are not below you. They stand as equals.

For context, Muslim forces first captured Jerusalem in 638 AD.

chapters.title: Seeing people brings peace, using them brings war
chapters.text:
Sorting people by race, culture, or faith into one flat box turns them into objects. Saladin’s humane treatment proves that even in the worst situations, like war, you can pick peace in your heart or war in your heart. That choice guides everything you do next. In a tough business negotiation, for instance, a negotiator who sees the other side as people will do better than one who sees them as obstacles to crush. The reason is simple: people do not enjoy dealing with jerks.

A peaceful heart lets you see clearly and decide well.

When you act on impulse, you pile up trouble for yourself. Skilled negotiators understand the other side’s concerns as well as their own. When your heart is at war, you answer objections with hostility. That hostility draws more hostility back, and the cycle keeps spinning.

Can you think of a moment when someone reacted unfairly to you even though you bent over backward for them? It happens because people respond to the way you act toward them, not just to your actions. You can sacrifice for your child, your partner, your employee, or your boss, but if your heart is at war while you sacrifice, they will not see the sacrifice. They will feel the way you carry yourself around them.

The trouble in our lives comes from a failure in our way of being, not from a bad plan.

chapters.title: Many fights are not fights at all, they are dances
chapters.text:
Collusion and conflict differ in a key way. Collusion is active, while conflict is passive. We pull failure toward us when our hearts are at war. By treating people as objects, we ask them to make our lives hard. We start to draw out of others the very things we claim to hate.

A fight where the two sides pull in the very things they say they oppose is called collusion.

A plain conflict might grow from a simple misunderstanding, but collusion comes from reacting to others in the same harsh way we feel they have acted toward us. This pattern grows without us seeing it.

Picture a wife who has asked her husband to mow the lawn. The husband would rather play tennis with his friend. His wife nags him about the lawn. The husband starts to see a demanding, unreasonable wife who nags all the time. He decides to protest by nagging her back. Even when he mows the lawn, he does it with scorn. In response, the wife sees a self-centered, inconsiderate, and childish husband.

Over time, both partners look for people who take their side and gather them as allies. This search for allies is how fights spread. Allies can be children, neighbors, and anyone in a similar situation.

When we gather allies, we start to paint others as demons and lump them into lifeless groups. Strangers become objects to us.

To break a conflict open, at least one side has to be willing to see how they may be wrong.

Believing you are right in a fight does not solve it. Real change starts when you look for the deeper fault in your own stance.

Your behavior toward others is the most important piece of being right or wrong.

Your actions or your position might be right, but you may be wrong in your way of being. Even if your attitude feels justified, ask whether your picture of the people you are acting toward is fair.

If we cannot stop the violence inside us, there is no hope of stopping the violence outside us, as the Arbinger Institute has written.

chapters.title: Peace starts with seeing how we picked war
chapters.text:
The idea of seeing people as either objects or humans forms the base for why we pick war instead of peace. The idea traces back to a thinker named Martin Buber. Long before Buber, the French philosopher Rene Descartes put forward the idea of a separate human consciousness that he called the I. Another philosopher, Martin Heidegger, pushed back on that view. He argued that the language Descartes used to frame the idea did not come from Descartes alone. So the case for a separate, lone I was weakened. We live in the world with other people. No one stands alone.

Buber, who lived in the same era as Heidegger, then proposed that we have two ways of being in the world. He called them I-Thou and I-It. In one mode, we see others as people. In the other, we see them as objects. But Buber did not answer the question of how we slide from I-Thou into I-It. To answer it, we have to look at how we gave up peace and chose war.

We choose war when we act against our own sense of what is right. That choice is a form of self-betrayal.

For example, when you refuse to help a person in need and tell yourself a story about why they do not deserve the help, you are betraying yourself. In that moment, you are choosing war.

We do not always do what we know is right. Sometimes we know we owe someone an apology, but we hold it back and build a story to justify the choice. When we betray ourselves this way, a new need grows inside us. We need to see others as the guilty party, and we start to care more about winning than about truth. That need is the need to feel justified.

When we see others as people, we want to help them. When we strip their humanity from them, we create a need to justify ourselves for breaking the truth about their humanity.

We puff up other people’s faults in our minds and hearts whenever we betray ourselves.

chapters.title: Four styles of self-justification are the seeds of war
chapters.text:
A heart at war makes others look terrible by blowing up their faults, the differences between them and us, and the importance of anything that helps us feel justified. Self-betrayal is the virus that poisons us. When we betray ourselves, we settle into a way of being that calls for constant self-justification. We twist everything we have been through to feed that feeling. The self, other people, the past, the present, the future, our hardships, our duties, and our view of the world all take on a new shape.

Our styles of self-justification differ from one person to another. Four common styles show up often.

The Better-than style is about feeling superior to others and judging people’s worth by the differences we notice between us and them.

The I-deserve style is about feeling mistreated, victimized, entitled, deprived, and a swarm of other heavy feelings.

The Need-to-be-seen-as style shows up when we are soft instead of demanding fairness or offering real help. For example, if you want to be seen as likable, you will hesitate to do the hard thing if the other person will not like you for it.

The Worse-than style is about seeing yourself as fated, broken, deficient, or not as good as others. When you find something you are not good at, you use it as a wall between you and other people. Their needs bounce off because you feel beneath them.

Self-betrayal is the act of going against your own sense of what is right toward another person, which lets you see them, yourself, and the world in a darker way.

Honoring a desire to help someone does not mean you will end up helping them. Still, the experience feels different when you cannot help from when you will not help. When you want to help but cannot, you start with the desire to help and end with the same desire. When you will not help, the desire fades, and you slip into one of the self-justification boxes to explain why you held back.

chapters.title: Your past shapes your favorite way to justify yourself
chapters.text:
People who grew up in a critical home often develop a Worse-than or Need-to-be-seen-as style. If you grew up in a wealthy or self-righteous family, you may lean toward Better-than and I-deserve styles.

No matter what our past looks like, we all try to find justification in some way. The good news is that we can unlearn these patterns. Even in the middle of a heated conflict, we can find a path to peace. First, we have to spot the ways we have built self-justification over the years.

Think about how the inner fight you carry has spilled out into outer fights with people in your life. Each of us has to look inward and ask where we may be pulling in the very behavior we complain about. When you react instead of respond, which justification box are you sitting in?

Notice your battles. Ponder your wars.

A heart at peace is the first move toward becoming an agent of peace.

chapters.title: You can invite change in others without forcing it
chapters.text:
When you see how tangled a situation is, you may feel like giving up and concluding that the fight cannot be solved. The best leaders and the best parents are the ones who climb out of the self-justification boxes. They stop casting themselves as victims and start inviting others to make the same positive changes they are making.

When we give up, we give up to a lie that says nothing can change. The justification wins. The better path is to pick the change you want to see. Take the first step toward healing by receiving other people as people in your heart, not as objects.

We drift apart at our own risk.

As humans, we need to notice how we can blame and justify against one person while being kind and gentle with another. That noticing gives us power because it shows we can leave the justification box behind for good. To do that, focus on building or holding onto experiences that let you see others as humans. When you feel stuck, take your mind to people and places that give you a clear view of the world. Talk to them or ask for help whenever you catch yourself blaming and justifying.

Experiences free of blame and self-justification help you crack problems that once felt impossible.

Fresh thoughts about a hard situation give you new angles you could not see before. As a result, you find an outward peace. A few questions can help you step out of self-justification.

What troubles this person or these people? How am I making things worse for them? Have I neglected or mistreated this person in other ways? What form of self-justification is hiding the truth about me and others and blocking solutions? Is there something I feel I should do for this person to help the situation?

Asking these questions does not shrink your pain. It helps you see how the other person or people are also hurting. That seeing forces you to set down your victimhood and notice new possibilities. Hope takes the place of anger and despair.

chapters.title: Inner peace must turn into action, not just thought
chapters.text:
Only you can choose the right action because only you have lived through the situation. Honoring the wish to help is how we stay out of our self-justification boxes.

In a personal conflict, a heart at peace plus action to improve things will cool the heat.

For conflicts with a long, painful history, a person whose heart is at peace can read the situation clearly without inflating it or explaining away their own part in it. Instead of stirring war, you become a force for peace. Some problems may be too tangled for a peaceful heart to solve, but they cannot be solved at all without starting from a peaceful way of being.

To spread the idea of peace, you need to help others change. The base of the influence pyramid deals with helping things go right, while the top of the pyramid deals with things that are going wrong. That pyramid gives a strategy for spreading peace.

Insisting that others change while ignoring our own need to adapt is wrong. Yet looking for fair corrections is not wrong.

While you are justifying your actions, you are likely to misread the situation. So the first step in helping things go right is to climb out of the self-justification box and gain a heart at peace. Next comes explaining the reasons for change. Correcting people without teaching them the why will fail. Asking people to leave their self-justification boxes without explaining what that means will also fall flat. Teaching and explaining reasons for change must be paired with listening and learning about the concerns of the people you are trying to reach. Learning keeps us humble and open to change, instead of assuming that only the people we are teaching need to change.

If we are poor learners, our teaching will fall flat, as the Arbinger Institute has written.

To learn from others, we have to build a relationship with them and with the people who shape their views. The influence pyramid is the cure for the conflicts and collusions we face at every level.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The book shows that we must call for peace by first gaining a heart of peace within. Hearts at war cannot build the right relationship that creates peace. Your heart is at peace when you see people as people, not as objects.

The Influence Pyramid lays out what we must do to settle conflicts and build a space where people can change. It has seven layers split into two levels.

The first level sits at the top and deals with things that are wrong. The second level forms the base and deals with helping things go right. The base contains six parts, and the top makes the seventh.

The parts of the base are teaching and communicating, listening and learning, building a relationship, building a relationship with people who shape the views of the people you want to change, and developing a heart of peace by spotting your self-justification boxes and leaving them behind.

To deal with things that are wrong, we have to correct them. To get good outcomes, we have to focus on the parts at the base. Here is the catch. We cannot correct things that are wrong if we do not first help things go right. Most fights drag on because one person spends time, energy, and resources trying to correct the other. We have to focus on the base instead. The pyramid tells us that the answer to a problem at any level always lives at a lower level. The base decides how well you can act at every level above. What is your way of being? Are you at peace or at war within you?

A behavior plan by itself cannot build a culture of change. A smart outward plan must match a peaceful inward plan.

Try this

Start at the base of the influence pyramid. Leave your self-justification box. Make a plan to stay out of the box and carry it out.

Read it to the end?
